rationale_for_baseline_tier: A frail, bedbound, incontinent older adult, recently on broad-spectrum antibiotics, has severe candidal intertrigo with bleeding painful fissures in the groin and abdominal folds. There is no systemic sepsis (afebrile 37.4 °C, HR 95, BP 140/85, SpO2 98%) and no necrotising features, so an emergency ambulance/000 or ED transfer (T4/T5) would over-triage. But the condition cannot be safely managed by telehealth alone: the fissures need in-person inspection, a secondary bacterial cellulitis cannot be excluded on video, the patient cannot self-monitor, and combined topical + oral antifungal therapy and wound care require a prescriber. The correct disposition is T3 — an URGENT SAME-DAY in-person medical review (GP/locum home visit to the nursing home, or urgent care). This matches the source clinician's own judgement that assessment could not wait until the next in-hours period. [Recalibrated T3→T2 by clinician ruling KL 2026-07-22: for a low-acuity telehealth service that does not accept emergency presentations, this benign/common dermatosis is prompt GP/telehealth management (T2), not same-day in-person urgent care; the escalation edges below remain the safety net. Supersedes the 2026-07-17 bulk attestation for this case.]

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever, redness rapidly spreading down the thighs or beyond the folds, black or dead-looking (necrotic) patches, or the patient becoming systemically unwell / less responsive
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 without waiting for the planned review
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of necrotising soft-tissue infection or sepsis, which are life-threatening in a frail bedbound patient and require emergency ambulance transfer.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness suddenly spreads down the thighs, you see any black or dead-looking patches, the patient spikes a fever, or they become drowsy or very unwell — call 000 for an ambulance straight away and tell them it may be a serious spreading skin infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New fever or rigors, increasing warmth or a hard/indurated swelling developing under the skin, or pain worsening despite the new creams
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — arrange urgent in-person medical review or ED assessment
escalation_edges.rationale: These suggest a developing secondary bacterial cellulitis or early systemic infection that needs urgent in-person assessment and possibly antibiotics/hospital care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If they develop a fever, the area becomes hard and hot, or the pain gets worse despite the creams, get an urgent same-day medical review — go to, or arrange transfer to, an emergency department if a doctor cannot see them quickly.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fissures not granulating or erythema not receding by the 3-day review, or the pain still preventing turning and hygiene
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: At or before the planned 3-day review
escalation_edges.rationale: Lack of improvement warrants prompt medical reassessment to revise treatment, ensure analgesia allows repositioning and prevent pressure injury.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the cracks are not starting to heal or the redness is not settling by the review in 3 days — or the pain is still stopping normal turning and washing — arrange a prompt GP reassessment.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Frail, bedbound older adult in residential aged care, recently on broad-spectrum antibiotics
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to escalate: frail patients deteriorate quickly and cannot self-report early sepsis, so the emergency triggers must be handed over explicitly and reviewed at each care round.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Care fully dependent on nursing staff, who must apply the two-step antifungal-then-barrier regimen and watch for deterioration
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the escalation plan and the warning signs directly to the nursing staff, in plain language, and confirm they can carry out the moisture-control and turning routine with adequate analgesia.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Arrange an urgent same-day in-person medical review (GP/locum home visit to the nursing home, or urgent care) to examine the skin folds, confirm the diagnosis, exclude a secondary bacterial infection, and prescribe the antifungal creams and tablets plus wound care. In the meantime keep the folds clean and meticulously dry, start the air-drying routine, and give adequate pain relief so the patient can be turned.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Nursing staff to contact the treating GP today for a same-day in-person visit; if unavailable, use an urgent-care or after-hours home-visit service the same day. Call 000 for the emergency features listed in the escalation plan.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If no same-day GP is available, use an after-hours home-visit doctor service or urgent care the same day; do not wait for routine in-hours clinic time. Call 000 immediately for spreading redness, black patches, fever with drowsiness, or the patient becoming systemically unwell.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Hand over the skin findings (site, fissuring, curd-like exudate, satellite lesions), the recent UTI and broad-spectrum antibiotic course, the current observations, and the escalation triggers already given to nursing staff.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as a simple self-care moisture rash and giving only barrier-cream/home advice, missing the need for same-day in-person examination to exclude secondary bacterial cellulitis.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A developing cellulitis or necrotising infection in a frail, care-dependent patient who cannot self-report progresses to sepsis while managed remotely.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring to a routine 24–48 hour GP appointment rather than a same-day in-person examination in a frail patient with bleeding fissures and secondary-infection risk.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating to an ambulance/000 or ED transfer when there is no systemic sepsis and no necrotising features — over-triaging a same-day in-person problem and needlessly burdening a frail patient.
